We're all good.
All right.
Sorry about that.
No worries.
You're very, very faint.
That's weird.
right.
right.
Thank

Yeah, there we go.
Now, whatever, we'll just do this.

don't know how familiar you are with North Carolina, but.
Awesome.

I'm in Rhode Island, so also, yeah.
You're nice.

We're a remote company.
There are fewer and fewer, but, yeah.
Cool.
Yeah, well, we could jump right in.
I can give you a little bit of background on GrowthX, the role.
We'll spend most of this time talking about you, pretty standard, and then should leave time for you to ask questions.
These are are
Usually like 30 minutes.
I think we'll be all set.
But if it ends early, it is not a reflection of, it is, ending early does not mean good or bad or anything.
It's just, there aren't that many questions.
Cool.
So yeah, growthx is, we are positioned as a marketing agency today, but really we're building a platform on the back end that our whole team uses to deliver content to, you know, to our clients.
So we're, it's AI powered workflows.
Eventually that platform will be externally facing.
And the structure of our team today is a pretty traditional like pod model for agencies.
So, and we're actually going through a reorg right now.
So I don't know when you talked to Tucker, but hopefully he kind of gave you a bit of a status update on kind of what this role is.
But up until maybe two to three weeks ago, directors were owning like a book of business.
We're pulling directors out of account ownership to just be kind of strategic execution experts.

So thinking about setting strategy, setting goals for clients, like kind of looking at the big picture and figuring out what a client needs to succeed, aligning like their KPIs to how we're going to execute against those goals and like kind of hit the contractual deliverables.
The pods are the people that are in the day-to-day execution, but you will kind of hop into an account.
We will have like a ticketing.
We do have a ticketing system where directors pick up a ticket.
They'll kind of like work with teams to do things like technical audits for SEO or...
Like, set a strategy, kind of look at, like, content refreshes, things like that.
But then there's engagement managers.
I, for right now, engagement managers are reporting to me.
Directors are reporting to Kyle, who I don't know what his title is at this point.
He's sort of been all over, but he did hold the director role for a little while.
So he's much more, like, on the strategic marketing side.
Engagement managers own, like, relationship and kind of day-to-day, like, project management, making sure that we're on track and kind of keeping an eye on performance.
So if something starts to slip, they'll pull in a director to support.

And then we have content managers and managing editors who are kind of that base layer, who are in the platform on a regular basis, generating content, and actually, like, working in the AI workflows.
I think things that are important for this role is like a willingness to get your hands dirty, interest in like coaching, though it's not a direct management role.

There's a lot, there's going to be a lot of like building playbooks, figuring out kind of what works and how you communicate that kind of like searching for success to other team members so that we can start to take a lot of that work out of director's hands.
Um, and then, yeah, just like the kind of execute fast and like prioritize well.
Um, so that's a little bit about growthx and the role.
Do you have any questions?
Not necessarily not about that yet.
No.
Okay.
Is that surprising to you at all in terms of like, what, what's what had been communicated to you before?
I'm always like, I don't know when the last time somebody talked to you was, so let's make sure you're aligned.
Um, I think I had it.
I had a conversation with Tucker maybe a week or two ago, so I guess he was spot on.
Great.
Awesome.
Okay, then let's talk about you.
Why don't you give me your elevator pitch, your experience, and we'll go from there.
Okay.
Yeah, sure.
I've been doing or been in SEO for around 10 years now.

Basically, I think I've done each kind of aspect or role within SEO, right, and kind of been the ownership of that, whether it's content, being the lead on tech, or being like that client management directorial kind of position where I'm at now.
In terms of types of clients, again, I think I've worked with them all.
I basically started out right out of college at a really small agency.
You know, we're talking five to 10 people, a lot of mom and pop kind of businesses, local businesses, things of that nature.

Moved up to an agency in which we worked with regional and some national, and now in my position now at Assembly Global, worked with a lot of national and international brands, a lot of Fortune 500 companies, things like that.
So basically, we had

I've seen, I think, and experienced it all in terms of what clients need, what can actually be done, and basically how to prioritize and execute the strategies properly to effectively achieve those goals.
Yeah, I'm certainly happy to explain my previous positions if you'd like, or would you rather me focus more so on my position now?
Well, let's talk about what you're doing now and maybe what you're looking for.
Sure.
Yeah, so in the senior director role right now, basically, I am the lead on three of the four of our largest enterprise SEO clients.
And what effectively that means is that I'm executing the strategy, executing the roadmap, overseeing the teams for those clients specifically, delegating the different tasks.

And then I'm also the lead on reporting and storytelling components to really see, you know, how we're doing, whether that's, you know, on a monthly cadence, the quarterly business reviews, semi, you know, however, whatever that cadence really is.
That's where a lot of my responsibility is.
So that's on the client side.

then outside of that, as a senior director at the company, I also have three directors that report directly to me, which my goal is, you know, personal development, you know, helping them with any client strategies or struggles they might have.
And then also as a senior director on the team, helping to improve and elevate our offering and really what we're going to market.

And, you know, that's really ramped up, especially with the evolution and adoption of AI search and kind of how we're changing our positioning from that kind of, you know, traditional SEO kind of offering to now shifting more into the AI search space.
But a lot of my time, you know, outside of clients, really making sure we are positioning ourselves as best as possible on the market.
Yeah.
Awesome.
Yeah.
Cool.
And then kind of you wear a lot of hats in that role.
Also, you mentioned like wanting to help that team grow, like what's, what excites you the most?
Like what part of the role makes you like the most.
So I really do like, I mean, I really love working with the clients that I have.
I'm very fortunate that the clients that I have, not only are they the largest ones, but they're also like good clients.
So, you know, helping to drive their success and develop those strategies is something that definitely excites me.

And being, you know, having earned the client's trust and, you know, seeing that success, they're definitely more willing to kind of take some risk or take some chances in which we're seeing, like, not necessarily risk per se, right?
But like, you know, to improve an SEO and especially in this AI search world, right?
It's about like, hey, let's test things.
Let's at least, you because we could definitely do things that are kind of the bread and butter that we know we're probably going to see success from.

But if we're trying to get from A to Z, you know, and we really want to branch out, we need to start testing different areas in which, you know, might not necessarily see the returns that we've seen historically.
So that type of thing excites me.
And then in terms of, you know, going for, you know, outside of the client and for the agency itself.
What excites me a lot is, you know, us really expanding our kind of cross-channel collaboration efforts.
So what we don't do well as an agency and kind of where we've kind of talked about positioning myself internally a lot is a lot of our channels can get isolated.
So, you know, SEO can work in a silo, our paid media can work in a silo, our PR efforts can work in a silo, which isn't an uncommon thing for a lot of agencies to see.

But what we're really trying to do right now, what I'm taking the lead on is, you know, helping to kind of be, I don't want to necessarily say the face of our offering per se, but like being the one that goes on like a roadshow or like, know, goes to the different leads in those channels and say, hey, this is what we're doing.
These are the strategies we're implementing.
This is how your channel impacts us.
And this is also how we can impact you and kind of helping engineer that like creative or cross-channel collaboration offering holistically to the entire agency and really helping drive that.
So that's kind of been my biggest excitement outside of like clients.
Yeah.
Can you talk to me about a strategic initiative that you've owned end-to-end?
Preferably something where you didn't have a lot of direction, how to drive the path forward yourself?
Sure.
Let's see.
So I'm just going to use a client name.
don't know if you can or not.
So for a really interesting thing that I've grown into is I work with Chanel and working with luxury clients can be super challenging in the SEO space, right?

They are very adverse to, quite frankly, doing a lot or taking those types of risks or doing anything outside of like anything that's not like protecting the brand is something they don't necessarily want to do, right?
So something that I've kind of engineered, right, that I've been pushing for and historically previous owners on the account really haven't gotten much traction on a lot of these efforts was
Helping them to say, hey, our non-branded search quite frankly sucks, and we're not really growing in this area, and we need to engineer and develop a strategy to do so.

So effectively what I did with the assistance of the team was look at their current performance metrics, their current product catalog, identifying the gaps in where the content on their site is in terms of them not targeting specific attributes, specific types of products that they do offer, but not from a non-branded standpoint.

And then developing a process at scale where we say, hey, we've done all the research, these are the types of queries and the types of terms we need to be targeting based upon the audience and the product selection that we have, and this is how we need to get it done.

And so basically, again, developing that research and then developing the roadmap as to, you know, how we need to create those pages, what also needs to go into those pages, and then reporting and develop or reporting on success, and then also creating like future phases to, you know, how to further kind of lean into this.
bulbs.
Yeah, how did you like get them to embrace that change in terms of their like the approach and positioning?
Yeah, so at first, I mean, honestly, just having really good client relationship and management goes a long way, right?

So again, building that trust, thinking about the, the reporting and storytelling that we're doing, like changing that completely, in a sense, and like making a lot of the metrics a lot more palatable and making sense to like what their KPIs are, and really what's driving growth.
And then really like framing everything as like, listen, you know, we're doing really well in terms of branded share of voice, like we're doing exceptional in that area.
But the biggest challenge that we continue to face is, you know, if branded search interest wanes, then there's nothing necessarily we can do to make up for that loss in interest, right?
So kind of like positioning a story differently.
And then like, you know, also obviously putting projections and things of that nature behind it.
And then also helping with priority, helping them like priority.
What's the highest level of impact versus the lowest?
And then what's the anticipated amount of resources that's going to be needed versus what are the lower quick wins?
Helping frame that all together really helped them take the step into that.
And so after positioning all of that, we ended up working with an outside vendor to help create these pages at scale.
And basically, we suggested doing a pilot for a specific division within their business.
Let that go for like a year.
Again, continually reporting on success, continuing to tell that story, continuing to show that.
And then from there, we got buy-in from the rest of the divisions who were way less receptive historically because we could point to specific instances of like, hey, this is where we're winning.
This is where we're seeing growth.

And, you know, they continue to see the same story that I told initially of like, we're doing well from a branded share of voice standpoint, but branded interest is going down or, you your competitors or X, and Z, know, whatever that might be.
So again, reinforcing that storyline with now proven results helped.
Get more buy-in.
What teams did you have to pull in internally?
Or also, I'm kind of curious about the outside vendor piece.
It's something that we have to do a lot with some of our more customer clients.
So how did you think about coordinating all of those pieces on your end?
So admittedly, we offered up vendors who we'd previously worked for that can help create pages at scale using reverse proxies and things like that.
But we weren't necessarily going to be the ones to do it.
But ultimately, at the end of the day, it's up to the client who they decide to go with.
We can only kind of make our recommendation.
So they ended up going with one of our recommendations just based on people that we worked with historically.
So there's that component of kind of helping with the vendor piece.
And then in terms of team members, we have our content team on our side.
So any type of content or optimizations like that that needed to be done.
that vendor the vendor piece piece the
We could work with them internally.
I, myself, just took the lead on the research component and the identifying the gap component and then leveraging that into the strategy.
And then also having, you know, other strategists on the team help from whether it's review, optimization, things like that.
We also had to work with their, they work in a way that they have a global team because, you know, being international, they have different regions.
So we do have to get buy-in from the global team.
So, again, it's replicating that similar story that we were telling to the U.S.
thing, getting their buy-in, which they are a tough cookie.
But we eventually got them to buy-in for that as well.
So we work with them.

And then in terms of working with the vendor, with all of our teams collaboratively, setting up either weekly or bi-weekly touch bases in which we're checking in on the statuses from, you know, obviously we have the research done.
Now we have to develop the pages.

Now we have to think about the copy that's going on the pages, optimization, the making sure that the different product feeds align with the keywords and the attributes that we're targeting and making sure that.
Those are all fulfilling and working in sync.
And then also thinking about post-launch, creating different plans around out-of-stock strategies, redirect strategies, things like that.
Okay, great.
Cool.
Can you talk to me about how you structure your work across different clients and prioritization over the course of a week?
So, you know, like you've got a bunch of clients, probably a couple of different deadlines.
Like, how do you structure your time?
What are your organization preferences?
Yeah, sure.
So we do have some project management tools that we use.
That definitely helps.
You know, I'll effectively, every year, I'll basically create a roadmap and then we'll kind of work out of that, right?
We'll have that roadmap function within our project management tool, as I'm sure you're aware.
In this industry, roadmaps that you created for.
Four months ago, don't always hold up, right?
So we always have to leverage some wiggle room for there to be not only ad hoc requests, but changes that occur in the industry.

Again, to your point, if a new priority pops up and we had something intended for March, but hey, this is really the priority and this is really what's driving growth, we kind of need to shift the plan that we've been doing.
So that's the foundation of kind of where everything lives in terms of how we structure those and then in terms of prioritizing.

So we'll have weekly or biweekly, depending on the client, touch bases with them just to, again, check up on status, check up on anything new, check up on their end, what priorities, what's going well, what's not going so well, things like that.
From there, we can have, again, some ad hoc requests or anything like that.
But basically, that's just like a status update to make sure we're all aligned.
In terms of, you know, how we're prioritizing things, basically, you know, with those tasks, I'll basically on my end help to, again, prioritize.
And then, you know, the expected kind of output from certain things, a lot of clients will certainly request that, right?

Because they kind of need to then take that back internally to then get buy-in from their team as to, you know, what we're really pushing, what's the top priority, what's going to have the most impact.
Once we align on that, then we move from there.

In terms of my time specifically, if it's, you know, something that is like foundationally, something like a foundational component to the strategy itself that like, I don't necessarily think I should just delegate as like an ad hoc task.
You know, I'll definitely take the lead on it.
Probably I'll ask for some support from the team.
But, you know, for example, right now, we're continuing to dive into our AI search offering and improving our client's visibility in AI search.

So like a foundational task for that would be mapping out like the different prompts that we're going to be tracking for a client, like thinking about the various product categories that make the most sense, the various stages of the user journey, the different attributes.

Which a user searches for in relation to those product categories, that's a task I wouldn't necessarily want to hand off because my strategy and everything that I do from there is going to come from that.
Again, I'll certainly ask for help because I can't do everything, right?
But in terms of, you know, if ad hoc tasks come in, I'll connect with the team.
I'll say, who has availability?
Let's do this.
If it's a larger request, you know, I'll probably pass that to a director and just make sure I'm connecting with them to ensure everything gets done.
But we have a pretty good system in which, you know, I'm able to track our team members' allocation so I can easily see, like, you know, do they have availability to handle this?
Connect with them, make sure.
And then basically, you know, during those check-ins, I'll just kind of get a status on how we're trending.
What tool are you guys using for your project management tracking?
We have monday.com.
Okay.
We are evaluating different tools right now.
We use...
And a couple of other things.
Yeah, I think I've used them all.
Monday is not my favorite.
Honestly, the agency itself, one of the weaknesses I basically said when I got here four years ago was we didn't have a strong project management platform or a lot of project managers.
So, like, basically all the SEOs had to basically be the PMs on all their accounts.
And some people are really good at it, but definitely we saw a lot of, like, things slipping through the cracks a lot of times.
So we basically all came together.
like, let's figure this out.
Let's take a little stress off everybody.
And we went with this.

So just curious about kind of what interested you about growthx.
So growthx and the company, I guess I'll talk company first before the role specifically.

You guys are answering the biggest challenge that we're facing right now and kind of, I don't know if frustration is the word, but definitely can get a little frustrated when we're working with clients around like content production.

And like, I was even watching, you know, the, the video of the, you know, the problem you guys are trying to solve and, you your CEO laid it out perfectly in terms of what that process looks like in terms of like, you know, you need to hire X amount of people and you need to bring on these people.
And then, you know, by the time you're getting ready to finally launch your content strategy, know, your six, seven.
Seven, eight months in, blah, blah, blah, blah, you the amount of time that went towards that.
So getting, you know, we're certainly trying to develop things on our side to help with that content production at scale.
You know, again, we have copywriters, but we'll help, we'll leverage AI to help with the workflows and really try to help produce things at a more efficient and a higher quality rate.
But we don't necessarily have the tools that GrowthX is offering.
So that problem being solved, I think, is really exciting.

You know, that's, I think, like the biggest opportunity for most of my clients is like, hey, we need to scale this content production because we see a lot of competitors or they might not even be direct competitors, but they're like competitors in the space because of how much copy or the type of copy they're producing and content they're producing.
I'm saying, hey, we're missing out because of this.
So being part of a solution, definitely love that.
But in terms of the job itself, yeah, everything strategy related definitely excites me and kind of being.

Being that driver of growth, you know, a lot of times I can get bogged down, I think, with some of the smaller day-to-day types of operations where, you I'd really like to just be kind of, again, in that strategic thinking kind of position.
Awesome.
Cool.

Do you have an example of maybe a playbook or something that you've built that the rest of the org uses today?
Yeah.
So, let's see.
So, I do have certain elements of that for reporting insights.

So, I've built out a tool that can, like, effectively, in Looker Studio, like, on a monthly basis, will, I've created different, like, views in which, like, we can look at, you know, is the driver of success or the driver for declines, right?
This is just kind of a simple example.
but...
Yeah.
Are the driver of declines, first off, mapping, like, the different pages or query types to be specifically for product categories or, like, content types.
So, again, grouping those together rather than looking at, like, full list of pages versus queries.
That's kind of, again, simplifying it down.
And then also looking at the data to say, you know, quick identifying, is this, is the growth or the decrease a component of search interest decreasing or increasing?
Is this more of a SERP change based upon what we're seeing from a click-through rate, but, like, ranking stability standpoint?
Or is this, you know, something else outside, external, whether it's, like, a paid component, right?
So I've created different workflows or different frameworks in that from a reporting standpoint.
Trying to think what else.
We've also developed ways to, you know, optimize our metadata for other clients at scale.
So, basically, know, kind of put that playbook together and that SOP of, like, helping people identify, like, what are the top...

Product categories in terms of revenue driving and of interest, how do I identify those SKUs that need to be optimized and then, you know, helping develop those prompts that we can then optimize that metadata at scale and then hopefully help launch that with a client.
So a couple of things we've helped develop just to, again, improve scalability and improve results for our clients.
Awesome.
Yeah.

think...

mean, everything Tucker was telling me, I was like, wow, this is so crazy, but.

I think, like, you know, we're hiring at every level because we, like, can't keep up with it.
So, like, the biggest challenge I'm facing right now is how do we actually start to, like, scale with the technology faster than the people?
Because right now we're, like, hiring just to keep up, but the tech is, like, we've got some agentic pipelines that we've.
That are doing like much, much better, but you know, there's the human in the loop is still important and will be important for a while.
So making sure that like people know how to leverage the tech is a big piece of it.
For sure.

Or like, what are the most common things?

Yeah, so the, that question gets to the heart of why we're reorging.
Because when we had directors over accounts, everybody got sucked into production.
Like you were like, okay, well, are we delivering your like X number of articles per week?
Great.
What do you think about them?
And it just, it made it really easy for clients to get nitpicky and for us.
To forget that our job, and especially the director's job, is to look up and around and bring new insights and energy to what clients are trying to do.
And so that's why we're pulling directors out of accounts, because we want them to have that objective bird's eye view in this, and be able to say, I don't have to join any calls today.

I have eight hours to think about like, either like, learn about what's happening, like what's changing in the AI landscape, or think about like, how we're going to repurpose something to better fit what we've learned about AI, or experiment with these three clients who are interested in doing X, Y, or Z.
Like to be a little bit more, we're putting out a bunch of content about like, being at the forefront of like AI, and like AI search, and how you can win that, even though there's no data.
about how you can win it.

just the testing and being transparent with them.

again, I think they, they appreciate that type of transparency, because, you know, we'll get a lot of questions from like, their leadership that are like, trying to be very black and white, or very specific coming from like, the historical SEO approach.

No, this looks awesome.
Yeah.
It's not, it's still a work in progress, but I haven't seen Marcel in, like, two weeks because he and Daniel, like, went into a room and just did this.
Cool.
Well, no, that's really it on my end.
Cool.
All right, David.
Well, it was great chatting with you.
And yeah, I think Tucker will follow up on Next Steps, but it's just, I think, one more screen interview and then a panel.
Okay.
Well, yeah, it was a pleasure meeting you as well.
And, you know, I hope you enjoy your Thanksgiving.
All right.
You too.
All right.
Thanks.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Screening interview for the Account Director role at GrowthX.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=223.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Candidate Profile:** David McDowell, a Senior Director at Assembly Global, has 10 years of SEO experience across small agencies to Fortune 500 clients.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=485.0)
  - [**Strategic Success:** McDowell secured buy-in from risk-averse luxury client Chanel for a non-branded SEO strategy by framing it as a hedge against declining branded search interest.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
  - [**Role Alignment:** The candidate is a strong fit for the new, strategic-only Director role, as he is excited to focus on high-impact strategy and leverage GrowthX's AI platform to solve client content scaling challenges.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1434.0)
  - [**GrowthX Context:** The company is reorging to shift Directors from account ownership to pure strategy, freeing them to build playbooks and conduct AI search experiments.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1894.0)

## Topics

### GrowthX's New Director Role & Reorg

  - [**Problem:** Directors were bogged down in production, preventing them from focusing on high-level strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1894.0)
  - [**Solution:** A reorg shifts Directors out of account ownership into a pure strategic execution role.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=289.0)
  - [**New Director Responsibilities:**](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=292.0)
      - [Set client strategy and goals.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=308.0)
      - [Align KPIs with contractual deliverables.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=316.0)
      - [Conduct strategic audits (e.g., technical SEO, content refreshes) via a ticketing system.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=340.0)
      - [Build playbooks to scale successful strategies across the team.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=420.0)
  - [**Team Structure:**](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=366.0)
      - [**Engagement Managers:** Own client relationships and project management.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=366.0)
      - [**Content Managers/Editors:** Execute content generation using the AI platform.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=401.0)

### Candidate Experience & Motivation

  - [**Current Role (Assembly Global):** Senior Director leading SEO for 3 of 4 largest enterprise clients.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=558.0)
      - [**Client Work:** Strategy, roadmap, and team oversight; reporting and storytelling.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=558.0)
      - [**Internal Work:** Manages 3 directors; leads cross-channel collaboration efforts; helps evolve the agency's AI search offering.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=558.0)
  - [**Motivation for GrowthX:**](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1434.0)
      - [**Platform:** Excited by the AI platform's ability to solve the client content scaling problem, a key challenge at his current agency.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1464.0)
      - [**Role:** Seeks a strategic-only position to focus on high-impact thinking, free from day-to-day operations.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1552.0)

### Strategic Initiative Example: Chanel

  - [**Challenge:** Secure buy-in from risk-averse luxury client Chanel for a non-branded SEO strategy.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
  - [**Approach:**](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=782.0)
    1.  [**Diagnosis:** Identified a major gap in non-branded search performance.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=829.0)
    2.  [**Framing:** Positioned the strategy as a hedge against declining branded search interest, not a risk.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=900.0)
    3.  [**Pilot:** Launched a successful 1-year pilot for one business division.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=973.0)
    4.  [**Scaling:** Used pilot results to secure buy-in from other divisions and the global team.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=973.0)
  - [**Execution:**](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1022.0)
      - [Coordinated internal teams (content, strategy) and an external vendor.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1022.0)
      - [Managed weekly/bi-weekly syncs to align on research, page development, and post-launch plans.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1118.0)

### Playbook & Process Development

  - [**Reporting Framework:** Built a Looker Studio tool to analyze performance drivers.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1583.0)
      - [**Function:** Maps success/decline to specific page/query types.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1594.0)
      - [**Insight:** Distinguishes between performance drops caused by search interest decline, SERP changes (e.g., CTR), or external factors (e.g., paid media).](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1633.0)
  - [**Metadata Optimization SOP:** Developed a playbook for scaling metadata optimization.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1664.0)
      - [**Process:** Identify top revenue-driving SKUs → create AI prompts for optimization → launch with client.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1666.0)

### Q\&A: GrowthX's Challenges & Tools

  - [**Primary Challenge:** Scaling the team to match the rapid pace of technology development.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1838.0)
  - [**Director Reorg Rationale:** To free up Directors for strategic thinking, experimentation, and playbook creation, moving them away from production management.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=1894.0)
  - [**AI Visibility Tools:**](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=2061.0)
      - [GrowthX uses Scrunch but is aware clients prefer Profound.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=2061.0)
      - [McDowell's agency also uses Scrunch but is considering switching to Profound due to data inconsistencies.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=2150.0)
      - [GrowthX is developing its own tool, "Check That," which leverages its internal AI workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=2100.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tucker:** Follow up with David McDowell on next steps (one more screen interview, then a panel).](https://fathom.video/share/nA4swswNv52jXkogGyEdWnPjph_n-tYS?tab=summary&timestamp=2191.0)
